evals.question_short: Watch backend memory-context tree + snapshot another backend's contexts

assertions.text: Uses the dev-cluster connection idiom: psql -h /tmp -d postgres (Unix socket, db postgres, no password)
Notes the role/$USER gotcha (superuser is your shell user, not literally 'postgres')
Queries pg_backend_memory_contexts ordered by total_bytes for the current backend
Uses \watch N to refresh the introspection query while a workload runs
Calls out that pg_backend_memory_contexts is per-connection (current backend only)
Uses pg_log_backend_memory_contexts(pid) to dump ANOTHER backend's contexts into the server log
Points at dev/data-debug/server.log (or /pg-tail-log) as the destination for the dump
Uses pg_stat_activity to find the target PID, filtering by backend_type='client backend' or by application_name
Mentions MessageContext (or another named context) as the place to watch growth for a per-message leak
Notes that the pg_log_* function requires PG 14+ (or otherwise calls out the version requirement)
Uses the CORRECT column set for pg_backend_memory_contexts (no reference to a non-existent 'parent' column; uses 'path' or omits the array)

evals.question_short: Capture backend PID and hand off to lldb without race

assertions.text: Uses the PGAPPNAME=hold env var to tag the holding session (NOT a \set variable)
Holds the backend with SELECT pg_sleep(N) so the PID stays alive long enough to attach
Looks up the PID from a SECOND psql session by querying pg_stat_activity WHERE application_name='hold'
Uses psql -At (unaligned, tuples-only) to make the PID-lookup output script-parseable
Uses -X to skip ~/.psqlrc so noisy rc output doesn't pollute the session
Hands the PID to /pg-attach (the project's lldb wrapper) rather than raw lldb -p
Notes that /pg-attach pre-sets breakpoints on errstart and MemoryContextStats
Connects to the local dev cluster on /tmp socket with db postgres (NOT a managed/network PG)
Notes that EVERY psql connection forks its own backend (per-connection-fork model), so the PID is the BACKEND's not psql's

evals.question_short: Force hash join, EXPLAIN (ANALYZE,BUFFERS,WAL), revert without restart

assertions.text: Uses SET enable_nestloop = off (and/or enable_mergejoin = off) to force the planner toward hash join
Notes that enable_* GUCs apply a huge cost penalty rather than HARD-disabling the node type
Uses EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, WAL, ...) — names BUFFERS and WAL specifically
Includes SETTINGS and/or VERBOSE in the EXPLAIN options for full context
Mentions FORMAT JSON for machine-parseable EXPLAIN output
Notes that SET is session-scoped and exiting psql resets (or RESET ALL / RESET <guc> reverts)
Distinguishes session SET from ALTER SYSTEM SET + pg_reload_conf for cross-session persistence
Mentions SET LOCAL inside BEGIN/COMMIT for txn-scoped GUC changes
Mentions \timing on for wall-clock measurement independent of EXPLAIN instrumentation
Uses the dev-cluster connection (psql -h /tmp -d postgres)

notes: Iter-2 added one harder assertion to eval 1: 'uses correct column set (no non-existent parent column)'. Both cohorts pass this on iter-2 because the bug was in the skill, not in baseline general knowledge — baseline never knew the column-name detail one way or the other and so simply doesn't reference it.
with_skill 30/30 vs iter-1 28/29 = lift recovered the one miss (per-connection-fork model) and survived a harder rubric (the path/parent column assertion).
baseline 11/30 vs iter-1 10/29 — essentially flat, accounting for the added assertion.
